title: Introduction
content:
# Introduction

docsfy is an open-source FastAPI service that generates polished, Mintlify-quality static HTML documentation sites from GitHub repositories using AI CLI tools. Point it at any repository — public or private — and docsfy clones it, sends an AI agent to analyze the entire codebase, and produces a fully rendered documentation site with navigation, search, syntax highlighting, and dark/light theme support.

## The Problem

Most software projects lack well-maintained, professionally formatted documentation. Writing docs is time-consuming, keeping them in sync with code changes is tedious, and producing a polished documentation site requires front-end skills that many backend developers don't prioritize. The result: outdated READMEs, scattered wiki pages, or no documentation at all.

## The Solution

docsfy automates the entire documentation lifecycle. It leverages AI CLI tools — Claude Code, Cursor Agent, or Gemini CLI — to read and understand your codebase, then generates structured, accurate documentation rendered as a static HTML site. When your code changes, docsfy detects the diff and regenerates only the affected pages.

```
POST /api/generate  <-- repo URL

  +----------+   +--------------+   +------------+   +----------+
  |  Clone   |-->|  AI Planner  |-->| AI Content |-->|   HTML   |
  |  Repo    |   |  (plan.json) |   | Generator  |   | Renderer |
  +----------+   +--------------+   +------------+   +----------+
```

## Key Features

- **AI-Powered Generation** — Uses AI CLI tools with full repository access to produce documentation that understands your code, not just its surface structure
- **Multiple AI Providers** — Supports Claude Code, Cursor Agent, and Gemini CLI through a unified provider interface
- **Incremental Updates** — Tracks commit SHAs and regenerates only pages affected by code changes
- **Static Site Output** — Generates self-contained HTML with sidebar navigation, client-side search (lunr.js), code syntax highlighting (highl

title: Quickstart
content:
# Quickstart

Get docsfy running in under 5 minutes with Docker Compose, generate your first documentation site, and view the results.

## Prerequisites

Before you begin, make sure you have the following installed:

- **Docker** (v20.10+) and **Docker Compose** (v2.0+)
- An API key for your chosen AI provider:
  - **Claude** — an `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` or Google Cloud credentials for Vertex AI
  - **Gemini** — a `GEMINI_API_KEY`
  - **Cursor** — a `CURSOR_API_KEY`

> **Tip:** Claude is the default AI provider. If you're unsure which to pick, start with Claude.

## Step 1: Clone the Repository

```bash
git clone https://github.com/your-org/docsfy.git
cd docsfy
```

## Step 2: Configure Environment Variables

Copy the example environment file and fill in your API key:

```bash
cp .env.example .env
```

Open `.env` in your editor. At minimum, set your AI provider credentials:

```bash
# AI Configuration
AI_PROVIDER=claude
AI_MODEL=claude-opus-4-6[1m]
AI_CLI_TIMEOUT=60

# Claude - Option 1: API Key
ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=sk-ant-...

# Logging
LOG_LEVEL=INFO
```

> **Note:** You only need to configure credentials for the provider you plan to use. Leave the other provider keys commented out.

### Alternative: Using Vertex AI for Claude

If you're running Claude through Google Cloud Vertex AI instead of a direct API key, configure these variables instead:

```bash
AI_PROVIDER=claude
CLAUDE_CODE_USE_VERTEX=1
CLOUD_ML_REGION=us-east5
ANTHROPIC_VERTEX_PROJECT_ID=your-gcp-project-id
```

When using Vertex AI, you also need to mount your gcloud credentials into the container (already configured in the default `docker-compose.yaml`).

## Step 3: Start docsfy

Launch the service with Docker Compose:

```bash
docker compose up -d
```

This builds the container image (first run only) and starts the docsfy service. The container:

- Runs on `python:3.12-slim` with all AI CLI tools pre-installed
- Exposes the API on port **8000**
- Persists generated documentation in a `./data` volume

Veri

title: Installation
content:
# Installation

docsfy can be installed using Docker (recommended), Docker Compose, or directly from source for local development.

## Prerequisites

Before installing docsfy, ensure you have the following:

- **Git** — for cloning repositories during documentation generation
- An API key or credentials for at least one supported AI provider:
  - [Claude Code](https://claude.ai) (default) — requires `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` or Vertex AI credentials
  - [Gemini CLI](https://github.com/google-gemini/gemini-cli) — requires `GEMINI_API_KEY`
  - [Cursor Agent](https://cursor.com) — requires `CURSOR_API_KEY`

## Docker (Recommended)

Docker is the recommended installation method. The image includes all system dependencies, AI CLI tools, and a non-root user configured for security.

### Build the Image

```bash
git clone https://github.com/your-org/docsfy.git
cd docsfy
docker build -t docsfy .
```

The multi-stage Dockerfile uses `python:3.12-slim` as the base image and installs:

- System dependencies: `bash`, `git`, `curl`, `nodejs`, `npm`, `ca-certificates`
- Python package manager: `uv`
- AI CLI tools: Claude Code, Cursor Agent, and Gemini CLI

### Run the Container

```bash
docker run -d \
  --name docsfy \
  -p 8000:8000 \
  -v ./data:/data \
  --env-file .env \
  docsfy
```

This maps port `8000` and mounts a local `./data` directory for persistent storage of the SQLite database and generated documentation sites.

### Verify the Installation

```bash
curl http://localhost:8000/health
```

A healthy instance returns a success response on the `/health` endpoint.

## Docker Compose

Docker Compose is the easiest way to run docsfy with all required volumes and configuration.

### 1. Create a `.env` File

Copy the environment template and configure your AI provider credentials:

```bash
cp .env.example .env
```

Edit `.env` with your settings:

```bash
# AI Configuration
AI_PROVIDER=claude
AI_MODEL=claude-opus-4-6[1m]
AI_CLI_TIMEOUT=60

# Claude - Option 1: API Key
ANTHROPIC_

title: Environment Variables
content:
# Environment Variables

Complete reference for all environment variables used to configure docsfy. These variables control AI provider selection, authentication, CLI behavior, and logging.

## Quick Reference

| Variable | Required | Default | Description |
|----------|----------|---------|-------------|
| `AI_PROVIDER` | No | `claude` | AI CLI provider to use |
| `AI_MODEL` | No | `claude-opus-4-6[1m]` | Model name passed to the AI CLI |
| `AI_CLI_TIMEOUT` | No | `60` | Maximum time (minutes) for AI CLI execution |
| `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` | Conditional | — | API key for Claude direct access |
| `CLAUDE_CODE_USE_VERTEX` | No | — | Enable Vertex AI authentication for Claude |
| `CLOUD_ML_REGION` | Conditional | — | Google Cloud region for Vertex AI |
| `ANTHROPIC_VERTEX_PROJECT_ID` | Conditional | — | Google Cloud project ID for Vertex AI |
| `GEMINI_API_KEY` | Conditional | — | API key for Gemini CLI |
| `CURSOR_API_KEY` | Conditional | — | API key for Cursor Agent |
| `LOG_LEVEL` | No | `INFO` | Application logging verbosity |

## Configuration File

docsfy loads environment variables from a `.env` file at the project root via the `docker-compose.yaml` `env_file` directive:

```yaml
services:
  docsfy:
    build: .
    ports:
      - "8000:8000"
    env_file: .env
```

Create your `.env` from the provided template:

```bash
cp .env.example .env
```

> **Warning:** Never commit your `.env` file to version control. It contains secrets such as API keys.

---

## AI Configuration

These variables control which AI CLI tool docsfy uses and how it behaves during documentation generation.

### `AI_PROVIDER`

Selects the AI CLI provider used for both the planning and content generation stages of the pipeline.

| | |
|---|---|
| **Type** | String |
| **Default** | `claude` |
| **Allowed values** | `claude`, `gemini`, `cursor` |

Each provider maps to a specific binary and command pattern:

| Provider value | Binary | Command template |
|----------------|--------|-------------

title: AI Providers
content:
# AI Providers

docsfy supports three AI CLI tools for generating documentation from your repositories. Each provider analyzes your codebase, plans the documentation structure, and writes the content. You can switch between providers at any time through environment configuration.

## Supported Providers

| Provider | CLI Binary | Best For |
|----------|-----------|----------|
| [Claude Code](#claude-code) | `claude` | Default provider; strong code analysis and structured output |
| [Gemini CLI](#gemini-cli) | `gemini` | Alternative provider with Google AI models |
| [Cursor Agent](#cursor-agent) | `agent` | Workspace-aware provider from the Cursor editor ecosystem |

## Configuration

All provider settings are controlled through environment variables. Set them in your `.env` file or pass them directly to the container.

### Core Settings

```bash
# AI Configuration
AI_PROVIDER=claude          # Provider name: claude, gemini, or cursor
AI_MODEL=claude-opus-4-6[1m] # Model identifier (provider-specific)
AI_CLI_TIMEOUT=60           # Timeout in minutes for AI CLI operations
```

| Variable | Default | Description |
|----------|---------|-------------|
| `AI_PROVIDER` | `claude` | Which AI provider to use |
| `AI_MODEL` | `claude-opus-4-6[1m]` | The model identifier passed to the CLI tool |
| `AI_CLI_TIMEOUT` | `60` | Maximum time (in minutes) for a single AI invocation |

> **Tip:** The timeout applies per AI invocation, not per page. Large repositories with many pages may require a higher timeout value since each page in the documentation plan triggers a separate AI call.

### Switching Providers

To switch providers, update `AI_PROVIDER` and `AI_MODEL` in your `.env` file:

```bash
# Switch to Gemini
AI_PROVIDER=gemini
AI_MODEL=gemini-2.5-pro
```

```bash
# Switch to Cursor Agent
AI_PROVIDER=cursor
AI_MODEL=claude-opus-4-6
```

Then restart the service for the change to take effect.

> **Note:** Each provider has its own authentication requirements. Make sure the app

title: Docker Deployment
content:
# Docker Deployment

docsfy is designed to run as a containerized service. This page covers the Dockerfile structure, docker-compose configuration, volume management, health checks, and production deployment best practices.

## Dockerfile Structure

docsfy uses a multi-stage build based on `python:3.12-slim` to keep the final image lean while including all necessary tooling.

### Base Image and System Dependencies

The container starts from `python:3.12-slim` and installs the system-level packages required for repository cloning, AI CLI tools, and health checks:

| Dependency | Purpose |
|------------|---------|
| `bash` | Shell for AI CLI installers |
| `git` | Cloning target repositories |
| `curl` | Health checks and CLI installer downloads |
| `nodejs`, `npm` | Gemini CLI runtime |
| `ca-certificates` | TLS for HTTPS git remotes and API calls |

### Package Management with uv

docsfy uses `uv` as its Python package manager — not pip. The entrypoint invokes `uv run --no-sync` to execute the application without re-resolving dependencies at startup:

```dockerfile
ENTRYPOINT ["uv", "run", "--no-sync", "uvicorn", "docsfy.main:app", "--host", "0.0.0.0", "--port", "8000"]
```

This starts the FastAPI application via uvicorn, listening on all interfaces at port 8000.

### AI CLI Installation

All three supported AI CLIs are installed at build time. They are unpinned to always pull the latest version:

```dockerfile
# Claude Code
RUN curl -fsSL https://claude.ai/install.sh | bash

# Cursor Agent
RUN curl -fsSL https://cursor.com/install | bash

# Gemini CLI
RUN npm install -g @google/gemini-cli
```

> **Note:** Because the CLI versions are unpinned, rebuilding the image at different times may produce containers with different CLI versions. Pin versions in production if reproducibility is critical.

### Non-Root User

The container runs as a non-root user `appuser` with OpenShift-compatible permissions (GID 0):

```dockerfile
RUN useradd -m -g 0 appuser
USER appuser
```

title: Generating Documentation
content:
# Generating Documentation

Documentation generation in docsfy is triggered through a single API call. You provide a GitHub repository URL, and docsfy clones the repository, uses an AI CLI tool to analyze the codebase, and produces a polished static HTML documentation site.

This page covers how to start generation, monitor progress, and access the finished docs for both public and private repositories.

## Quick Start

To generate documentation for a repository, send a `POST` request to the `/api/generate` endpoint with the repository URL:

```bash
curl -X POST http://localhost:8000/api/generate \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"url": "https://github.com/your-org/your-repo"}'
```

docsfy will clone the repository and run through the full generation pipeline. You can monitor progress via the status endpoints and access the finished site once generation completes.

## Generation Pipeline

Every generation request passes through four sequential stages:

### Stage 1: Clone Repository

docsfy performs a shallow clone (`--depth 1`) of the repository to a temporary directory. This keeps clone times fast, even for large repositories with extensive commit histories.

### Stage 2: AI Planner

The configured AI CLI runs with its working directory set to the cloned repo. It explores the entire repository and produces a `plan.json` file containing the documentation structure — pages, sections, and navigation hierarchy.

### Stage 3: AI Content Generator

For each page defined in `plan.json`, the AI CLI runs again with access to the full codebase. Pages are generated concurrently using async execution with semaphore-limited concurrency. Each page is output as a Markdown file and cached at `/data/projects/{name}/cache/pages/*.md`.

### Stage 4: HTML Renderer

Markdown pages and `plan.json` are converted into a polished static HTML site using Jinja2 templates with bundled CSS/JS assets. The final site includes sidebar navigation, dark/light theme toggle, client-sid

title: Serving Documentation
content:
# Serving Documentation

docsfy provides two ways to access your generated documentation: serve it directly from the docsfy API, or download it as a `.tar.gz` archive for self-hosting on your own infrastructure.

## Serving Directly from docsfy

Once documentation has been generated for a project, docsfy serves the rendered static HTML site automatically through its built-in FastAPI server.

### Accessing Served Documentation

Generated documentation is available at:

```
GET /docs/{project}/{path}
```

where `{project}` is the project name and `{path}` is the page path within the generated site. For example, if you generated docs for a project named `my-app`:

```
http://localhost:8000/docs/my-app/
http://localhost:8000/docs/my-app/index.html
http://localhost:8000/docs/my-app/getting-started.html
```

The served site includes all rendered assets — CSS, JavaScript, search index, and syntax highlighting — so no additional setup is required.

### Starting the Server

docsfy runs as a FastAPI application served by uvicorn. The default entrypoint starts the server on all interfaces at port 8000:

```bash
uv run --no-sync uvicorn docsfy.main:app --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8000
```

With Docker Compose, this is handled automatically:

```yaml
services:
  docsfy:
    build: .
    ports:
      - "8000:8000"
    env_file: .env
    volumes:
      - ./data:/data
      - ~/.config/gcloud:/home/appuser/.config/gcloud:ro
      - ./cursor:/home/appuser/.config/cursor
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD", "curl", "-f", "http://localhost:8000/health"]
      interval: 30s
      timeout: 10s
      retries: 3
```

> **Tip:** Use the `GET /health` endpoint to verify the server is running and ready to serve requests.

### Verifying Project Status

Before accessing served documentation, confirm the project has finished generating:

```bash
curl http://localhost:8000/api/projects/my-app
```

The response includes the project status. Documentation is only servable when the status is `ready`:

| S

title: Incremental Updates
content:
# Incremental Updates

docsfy uses an incremental update strategy to avoid regenerating entire documentation sites when only part of a repository has changed. By tracking commit SHAs and comparing documentation plans, docsfy intelligently determines which pages need regeneration — saving time, reducing AI costs, and delivering faster updates.

## How It Works

The incremental update pipeline follows a layered change-detection approach. Rather than regenerating every page on each request, docsfy compares the current repository state against the last successful generation and narrows down the work to only what has changed.

```
POST /api/generate (repo URL)
        │
        v
┌─────────────────────────┐
│  Fetch repo & get HEAD  │
│  commit SHA             │
└────────────┬────────────┘
             │
             v
┌─────────────────────────┐     match
│  Compare SHA against    ├────────────► No changes — skip generation
│  stored SHA in SQLite   │
└────────────┬────────────┘
             │ different
             v
┌─────────────────────────┐     unchanged
│  Re-run AI Planner      ├────────────► Regenerate only affected pages
│  Compare new plan.json  │
│  against cached plan    │
└────────────┬────────────┘
             │ changed
             v
┌─────────────────────────┐
│  Full regeneration      │
│  (new structure)        │
└─────────────────────────┘
```

The process breaks down into three layers of detection:

1. **Commit SHA comparison** — Has the repository changed at all?
2. **Plan structure comparison** — Has the documentation structure changed?
3. **Affected page identification** — Which specific pages need new content?

## Commit SHA Tracking

Every time docsfy generates documentation for a project, it records the HEAD commit SHA of the repository in its SQLite database. This SHA serves as the primary checkpoint for detecting changes.

### Storage

The commit SHA is stored alongside other project metadata in the SQLite database at `/data/docsfy.db`:

```

title: Generation Pipeline
content:
# Generation Pipeline

docsfy transforms a GitHub repository into a polished static documentation site through a four-stage sequential pipeline. Each stage builds on the output of the previous one, progressing from raw source code to finished HTML.

```
 ┌──────────────┐    ┌──────────────┐    ┌──────────────┐    ┌──────────────┐
 │   Stage 1    │    │   Stage 2    │    │   Stage 3    │    │   Stage 4    │
 │  Clone Repo  │───>│  AI Planner  │───>│  AI Content  │───>│    HTML      │
 │              │    │  (plan.json) │    │  Generator   │    │  Renderer    │
 └──────────────┘    └──────────────┘    └──────────────┘    └──────────────┘
       │                    │                    │                    │
       v                    v                    v                    v
   temp directory       plan.json          cache/pages/*.md     site/*.html
```

The entire pipeline is triggered by a single API call:

```bash
curl -X POST http://localhost:8000/api/generate \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"repo_url": "https://github.com/org/repo"}'
```

---

## Stage 1: Clone Repository

The pipeline begins by cloning the target repository to a temporary directory on disk. This gives the AI full filesystem access to every file in the project.

### How It Works

docsfy performs a **shallow clone** (`--depth 1`) to minimize disk usage and clone time. Only the latest commit on the default branch is fetched — full history is not needed for documentation generation.

```bash
git clone --depth 1 <repo_url> <temp_directory>
```

### Repository Access

Both public and private repositories are supported:

| URL Format | Access Type | Authentication |
|------------|-------------|----------------|
| `https://github.com/org/repo` | Public | None required |
| `https://github.com/org/repo` | Private | System git credentials |
| `git@github.com:org/repo.git` | Private (SSH) | SSH keys on the host |

Private repository access relies on the git credentials available in the

title: AI CLI Integration
content:
# AI CLI Integration

docsfy delegates all intelligence — repository analysis, documentation planning, and content generation — to external AI CLI tools running as subprocesses. This page explains the provider abstraction layer, how commands are built and executed, how AI responses are parsed, and how timeouts protect the pipeline from hung processes.

## Overview

Rather than embedding AI SDKs or calling HTTP APIs directly, docsfy shells out to locally-installed AI CLI binaries. This approach offers several advantages:

- **No SDK dependencies** — the AI tools manage their own authentication, model routing, and API versioning
- **Provider portability** — switching from Claude to Gemini or Cursor requires changing a single environment variable
- **Full repository context** — each CLI tool runs with `cwd` set to the cloned repository, giving it native file exploration capabilities

The integration layer follows the same provider pattern used in [jenkins-job-insight](https://github.com/), keeping the abstraction consistent across projects in the ecosystem.

## The ProviderConfig Pattern

At the core of the integration is a frozen dataclass that encapsulates everything docsfy needs to know about a given AI CLI tool:

```python
@dataclass(frozen=True)
class ProviderConfig:
    binary: str
    build_cmd: Callable
    uses_own_cwd: bool = False
```

### Field Reference

| Field | Type | Description |
|-------|------|-------------|
| `binary` | `str` | Name of the CLI executable (e.g., `"claude"`, `"gemini"`, `"agent"`) |
| `build_cmd` | `Callable` | Function that constructs the full command-line argument list given a model name and optional workspace path |
| `uses_own_cwd` | `bool` | Whether the provider handles working directory via its own flag (e.g., `--workspace`) rather than subprocess `cwd`. Defaults to `False` |

> **Note:** The dataclass uses `frozen=True`, making provider configurations immutable after construction. This prevents accidental mutation during concu

title: Storage & Database
content:
# Storage & Database

docsfy uses a two-layer storage architecture: a **SQLite database** for project metadata and tracking, and a **filesystem directory tree** for cached markdown content, rendered HTML sites, and search indexes. Both live under a single `/data` mount point for simple container deployment.

## Storage Overview

```
/data/
  docsfy.db                          # SQLite database (project metadata)
  projects/                          # Filesystem storage (generated content)
    {project-name}/
      plan.json                      # Documentation structure from AI Planner
      cache/
        pages/*.md                   # AI-generated markdown (cached)
      site/                          # Final rendered static HTML site
        index.html
        *.html
        assets/
          style.css
          search.js
          theme-toggle.js
          highlight.js
        search-index.json            # Client-side search index
```

All storage is rooted at `/data`, which is mapped as a Docker volume:

```yaml
# docker-compose.yaml
services:
  docsfy:
    volumes:
      - ./data:/data
```

> **Tip:** Since all persistent state lives under `/data`, backing up this single directory captures everything — the database, cached content, and rendered sites.

## SQLite Database

### Location

The database file lives at `/data/docsfy.db`. docsfy accesses it asynchronously via `aiosqlite`, which is well-suited for the FastAPI async request lifecycle.

### Schema

The database stores **project metadata only** — it does not store page content or rendered HTML. The tracked fields are:

| Field | Description |
|-------|-------------|
| Project name | Unique identifier derived from the repository |
| Repository URL | Source GitHub repository (SSH or HTTPS) |
| Status | Current generation state: `generating`, `ready`, or `error` |
| Last generated | Timestamp of the most recent successful generation |
| Last commit SHA | HEAD commit SHA from the last generation run |
| Gene

title: HTML Renderer
content:
# HTML Renderer

The HTML Renderer is Stage 4 of the docsfy generation pipeline. It transforms AI-generated Markdown pages and the `plan.json` navigation structure into a polished, self-contained static HTML documentation site.

## Overview

The renderer takes two inputs:

1. **`plan.json`** — the documentation structure produced by the AI Planner (Stage 2), defining pages, sections, and navigation hierarchy
2. **Markdown files** — per-page content generated by the AI Content Generator (Stage 3), cached at `/data/projects/{name}/cache/pages/*.md`

It produces a complete static site at `/data/projects/{name}/site/` that can be served directly by the docsfy API or downloaded as a `.tar.gz` archive for self-hosting.

```
/data/projects/{project-name}/site/
  index.html
  *.html
  assets/
    style.css
    search.js
    theme-toggle.js
    highlight.js
  search-index.json
```

## Jinja2 Templating

The renderer uses [Jinja2](https://jinja.palletsprojects.com/) as its templating engine to convert Markdown content into fully-structured HTML pages. Each page defined in `plan.json` is rendered through a base template that provides consistent layout, navigation, and asset loading.

### Template Context

When rendering a page, the following context variables are passed to the Jinja2 template:

| Variable | Type | Description |
|----------|------|-------------|
| `page_title` | `str` | Title of the current page |
| `page_content` | `str` | Rendered HTML from Markdown source |
| `project_name` | `str` | Name of the documentation project |
| `navigation` | `list[dict]` | Sidebar navigation tree from `plan.json` |
| `current_page` | `str` | Slug/path of the active page (for highlighting) |
| `pages` | `list[dict]` | All pages metadata for search index generation |

### Markdown Processing

Markdown content is converted to HTML using the Python `markdown` library before being injected into the template. The renderer processes several extended Markdown features:

- **Fenced code bl

title: POST /api/generate
content:
# POST /api/generate

Start documentation generation for a GitHub repository. This endpoint triggers the full generation pipeline — cloning the repository, planning the documentation structure with AI, generating page content, and rendering a polished static HTML site.

## Endpoint

```
POST /api/generate
```

## Request Body

Send a JSON object containing the GitHub repository URL to generate documentation for.

```json
{
  "repo_url": "https://github.com/owner/repository"
}
```

### Parameters

| Field | Type | Required | Description |
|-------|------|----------|-------------|
| `repo_url` | `string` | Yes | The GitHub repository URL. Supports both HTTPS and SSH formats. |

### Supported URL Formats

Both public and private repositories are supported:

```bash
# HTTPS (public or private)
https://github.com/owner/repository

# SSH (private repos using system git credentials)
git@github.com:owner/repository.git
```

> **Note:** Private repository access relies on system git credentials configured in the container environment. See [Configuration](/configuration) for details on mounting credentials.

## Response

A successful request returns `200 OK` with the project metadata, including its current generation status.

```json
{
  "name": "repository",
  "repo_url": "https://github.com/owner/repository",
  "status": "generating",
  "last_generated": null,
  "last_commit_sha": null
}
```

### Response Fields

| Field | Type | Description |
|-------|------|-------------|
| `name` | `string` | The project name, derived from the repository name. |
| `repo_url` | `string` | The repository URL that was submitted. |
| `status` | `string` | Current generation status. One of `generating`, `ready`, or `error`. |
| `last_generated` | `string \| null` | ISO 8601 timestamp of the last successful generation, or `null` if not yet complete. |
| `last_commit_sha` | `string \| null` | The commit SHA used for the most recent generation, or `null` if not yet complete. |

### Status Values

title: GET /api/status
content:
# GET /api/status

List all projects and their current documentation generation status.

## Overview

The `GET /api/status` endpoint returns a summary of every project registered in docsfy along with its current generation state. Use this endpoint to monitor ongoing generation jobs, verify completed builds, or detect failures across all your documentation projects at a glance.

## Request

```
GET /api/status
```

No query parameters, request body, or authentication are required.

### Example Request

```bash
curl http://localhost:8000/api/status
```

```python
import httpx

response = httpx.get("http://localhost:8000/api/status")
projects = response.json()
```

## Response

Returns a JSON array of project objects. Each object contains the project name, its source repository, and the current generation status.

### Response Fields

| Field | Type | Description |
|-------|------|-------------|
| `name` | `string` | Unique project identifier derived from the repository name |
| `repo_url` | `string` | GitHub repository URL used for generation |
| `status` | `string` | Current generation status: `generating`, `ready`, or `error` |
| `last_generated` | `string \| null` | ISO 8601 timestamp of the last successful generation, or `null` if never completed |
| `last_commit_sha` | `string \| null` | Git commit SHA from the most recent generation run |

### Status Values

| Status | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| `generating` | The documentation pipeline is actively running. The project is being cloned, planned, or rendered. |
| `ready` | Generation completed successfully. The static HTML site is available to serve or download. |
| `error` | Generation failed at some stage. Check project details for logs. |

### Example Response

```json
[
  {
    "name": "my-api",
    "repo_url": "https://github.com/acme/my-api",
    "status": "ready",
    "last_generated": "2026-03-04T14:32:10Z",
    "last_commit_sha": "a1b2c3d4e5f6"
  },
  {
    "name": "frontend-sdk",
    "repo_

title: GET /api/projects/{name}
content:
# GET /api/projects/{name}

Retrieve detailed information about a specific documentation project, including its current status, the last generated timestamp, the commit SHA used for generation, and the full list of generated pages.

## Endpoint

```
GET /api/projects/{name}
```

## Path Parameters

| Parameter | Type | Required | Description |
|-----------|------|----------|-------------|
| `name` | `string` | Yes | The project name. This matches the directory name under `/data/projects/`. |

## Response

### 200 OK

Returns the full project details.

```json
{
  "name": "docsfy",
  "repo_url": "https://github.com/username/docsfy.git",
  "status": "ready",
  "last_generated": "2026-03-04T14:32:10Z",
  "last_commit_sha": "c6db255a9f1e4b8d7c3a2e6f0b1d5c8a4e7f9b2d",
  "pages": [
    {
      "title": "Introduction",
      "path": "index.html"
    },
    {
      "title": "Getting Started",
      "path": "getting-started.html"
    },
    {
      "title": "API Reference",
      "path": "api-reference.html"
    },
    {
      "title": "Configuration",
      "path": "configuration.html"
    }
  ]
}
```

### Response Fields

| Field | Type | Description |
|-------|------|-------------|
| `name` | `string` | The project name. |
| `repo_url` | `string` | The source GitHub repository URL (SSH or HTTPS). |
| `status` | `string` | Current project status. One of `generating`, `ready`, or `error`. |
| `last_generated` | `string` | ISO 8601 timestamp of the most recent successful generation. `null` if the project has never completed generation. |
| `last_commit_sha` | `string` | The full Git commit SHA that was used for the last generation. Used internally for [incremental updates](#incremental-updates). `null` if no generation has completed. |
| `pages` | `array` | List of pages in the generated documentation site, derived from the project's `plan.json`. Empty array if generation is still in progress. |

### Project Status Values

| Status | Description |
|--------|-------------|
|

title: DELETE /api/projects/{name}
content:
# DELETE /api/projects/{name}

Remove a project and all its generated documentation from the server. This permanently deletes the project metadata from the SQLite database and removes all associated files from the filesystem, including cached markdown, rendered HTML, and the documentation plan.

## Endpoint

```
DELETE /api/projects/{name}
```

## Path Parameters

| Parameter | Type | Required | Description |
|-----------|------|----------|-------------|
| `name` | `string` | Yes | The unique name of the project to delete. This is the project name assigned during generation via `POST /api/generate`. |

## Response

### 200 OK

The project and all associated documentation were successfully deleted.

```json
{
  "status": "deleted",
  "project": "my-project"
}
```

### 404 Not Found

The specified project does not exist.

```json
{
  "detail": "Project 'my-project' not found"
}
```

## Examples

### cURL

```bash
curl -X DELETE http://localhost:8000/api/projects/my-project
```

### Python (requests)

```python
import requests

response = requests.delete("http://localhost:8000/api/projects/my-project")

if response.status_code == 200:
    print(f"Project deleted: {response.json()}")
elif response.status_code == 404:
    print(f"Project not found: {response.json()['detail']}")
```

### Python (httpx, async)

```python
import httpx

async with httpx.AsyncClient() as client:
    response = await client.delete("http://localhost:8000/api/projects/my-project")
    response.raise_for_status()
    print(response.json())
```

## What Gets Deleted

When a project is deleted, the following resources are permanently removed:

### Database Records

Project metadata is deleted from the SQLite database at `/data/docsfy.db`, including:

- Project name and repository URL
- Generation status (`generating` / `ready` / `error`)
- Last generated timestamp and commit SHA
- Generation history and logs

### Filesystem

The entire project directory at `/data/projects/{name}/` is removed, which

title: GET /api/projects/{name}/download
content:
# GET /api/projects/{name}/download

Download the complete generated documentation site as a `.tar.gz` archive for self-hosting.

## Overview

This endpoint packages the fully rendered static HTML documentation site for a project into a compressed `.tar.gz` archive. Use it to download your generated docs and host them on any static file server — Nginx, Apache, S3, GitHub Pages, Netlify, or any other platform that serves static files.

## Endpoint

```
GET /api/projects/{name}/download
```

### Path Parameters

| Parameter | Type | Required | Description |
|-----------|------|----------|-------------|
| `name` | `string` | Yes | The project name used during generation |

## Request

No request body or query parameters are required. The endpoint only needs the project name in the URL path.

### Example Request

```bash
curl -O -J http://localhost:8000/api/projects/my-project/download
```

The `-O` flag saves the file using the server-provided filename, and `-J` uses the `Content-Disposition` header for the filename.

### Saving to a Specific Path

```bash
curl -o my-project-docs.tar.gz http://localhost:8000/api/projects/my-project/download
```

## Response

### Success (200 OK)

Returns a binary `.tar.gz` archive containing the complete static documentation site.

**Response Headers:**

| Header | Value |
|--------|-------|
| `Content-Type` | `application/gzip` |
| `Content-Disposition` | `attachment; filename="{name}-docs.tar.gz"` |

### Error Responses

| Status Code | Description |
|-------------|-------------|
| `404 Not Found` | Project does not exist |
| `409 Conflict` | Project documentation is still generating (status is `generating`) |
| `500 Internal Server Error` | Archive creation failed |

## Archive Contents

The downloaded archive mirrors the rendered site directory structure stored at `/data/projects/{name}/site/`:

```
{name}-docs/
├── index.html              # Landing page
├── getting-started.html    # Generated documentation pages
├── api-reference.

title: GET /docs/{project}/{path}
content:
# GET /docs/{project}/{path}

Serve generated static HTML documentation pages directly from the docsfy server.

## Overview

The `GET /docs/{project}/{path}` endpoint serves the final rendered HTML documentation for a project. After docsfy's generation pipeline produces a static site, this endpoint makes it immediately browsable — no separate web server or download step required.

Each project's documentation lives under its own URL namespace, making it easy to host multiple projects from a single docsfy instance.

## URL Parameters

| Parameter | Type | Description |
|-----------|------|-------------|
| `project` | `string` | The project name (derived from the repository name during generation) |
| `path` | `string` | The path to a specific file within the generated site (e.g., `index.html`, `getting-started.html`, `assets/style.css`) |

## How It Works

When a request hits `/docs/{project}/{path}`, docsfy resolves the path against the project's generated site directory on the filesystem:

```
/data/projects/{project}/site/{path}
```

This directory is populated by Stage 4 (HTML Renderer) of the generation pipeline. The endpoint serves whatever file matches the requested path — HTML pages, CSS stylesheets, JavaScript files, search indexes, and any other static assets.

### Request Flow

```
Client Request
    GET /docs/my-project/getting-started.html
         │
         ▼
    FastAPI Router
         │
         ▼
    Resolve filesystem path
    /data/projects/my-project/site/getting-started.html
         │
         ▼
    Return file with appropriate Content-Type
```

## Usage Examples

### Browse a Project's Documentation

Navigate to the root page of a project's documentation:

```
GET /docs/my-project/index.html
```

### Access a Specific Page

Each page defined in the project's `plan.json` becomes an HTML file in the site directory:

```
GET /docs/my-project/getting-started.html
GET /docs/my-project/api-reference.html
GET /docs/my-project/configuration.html
```

title: GET /health
content:
# GET /health

Health check endpoint for container orchestration and monitoring.

## Overview

The `/health` endpoint provides a lightweight health check for the docsfy service. It is designed for use by container orchestration platforms (Docker, Kubernetes, OpenShift) and external monitoring systems to verify that the FastAPI server is running and accepting requests.

## Request

```
GET /health
```

No authentication, query parameters, or request body required.

## Response

**Status Code:** `200 OK`

```json
{"status": "ok"}
```

The endpoint returns a minimal JSON response confirming the service is operational. By design, this is a shallow health check — it verifies that the FastAPI application and uvicorn server are responsive without probing backend dependencies like the SQLite database or filesystem storage.

> **Note:** This endpoint does not validate connectivity to `/data/docsfy.db` or the `/data/projects/` filesystem. It confirms only that the HTTP server is accepting connections on port 8000.

## Usage Examples

### curl

```bash
curl -f http://localhost:8000/health
```

### Python (requests)

```python
import requests

response = requests.get("http://localhost:8000/health")
response.raise_for_status()
print(response.json())  # {"status": "ok"}
```

### Python (httpx, async)

```python
import httpx

async with httpx.AsyncClient() as client:
    response = await client.get("http://localhost:8000/health")
    response.raise_for_status()
    print(response.json())  # {"status": "ok"}
```

## Docker Health Check

The `docker-compose.yaml` configures automatic health checking against this endpoint:

```yaml
services:
  docsfy:
    build: .
    ports:
      - "8000:8000"
    env_file: .env
    volumes:
      - ./data:/data
      - ~/.config/gcloud:/home/appuser/.config/gcloud:ro
      - ./cursor:/home/appuser/.config/cursor
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD", "curl", "-f", "http://localhost:8000/health"]
      interval: 30s
      timeout: 10s
      retries: 3

title: Contributing
content:
# Contributing

## Prerequisites

docsfy requires the following tools installed on your development machine:

- **Python 3.12+**
- **[uv](https://docs.astral.sh/uv/)** — the sole package manager for this project (do not use pip)
- **Git**

> **Warning:** This project uses `uv` exclusively for dependency management. Do not use `pip`, `pip-tools`, or other package managers. All commands assume `uv` is available on your `PATH`.

## Development Setup

### 1. Clone the Repository

```bash
git clone https://github.com/your-org/docsfy.git
cd docsfy
```

### 2. Create a Virtual Environment and Install Dependencies

```bash
uv venv
uv sync --dev
```

This installs all runtime and development dependencies defined in `pyproject.toml`, including linters, type checkers, and test frameworks.

### 3. Install Pre-commit Hooks

```bash
uv run pre-commit install
```

This registers the pre-commit hooks so they run automatically on every `git commit`. You can also run them manually against all files:

```bash
uv run pre-commit run --all-files
```

### 4. Set Up Environment Variables

Copy the example environment file and configure it for local development:

```bash
cp .env.example .env
```

At minimum, configure an AI provider for local testing:

```bash
# AI Configuration
AI_PROVIDER=claude
AI_MODEL=claude-opus-4-6[1m]
AI_CLI_TIMEOUT=60

# Claude - Option 1: API Key
# ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=

# Claude - Option 2: Vertex AI
# CLAUDE_CODE_USE_VERTEX=1
# CLOUD_ML_REGION=
# ANTHROPIC_VERTEX_PROJECT_ID=

# Gemini
# GEMINI_API_KEY=

# Cursor
# CURSOR_API_KEY=

# Logging
LOG_LEVEL=INFO
```

### 5. Run the Development Server

```bash
uv run uvicorn docsfy.main:app --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8000 --reload
```

The API will be available at `http://localhost:8000` with auto-reload on code changes. The health check endpoint is at `http://localhost:8000/health`.

## Build System

docsfy uses **hatchling** as its build backend. The project metadata and build configuration are defined in `pyproject.toml`:

`

title: Technology Stack
content:
# Technology Stack

## Overview

docsfy is built on a modern Python stack designed for async operation, lightweight deployment, and zero-dependency static output. The service combines a FastAPI backend with Jinja2 templating and SQLite metadata storage to orchestrate AI-driven documentation generation and serve polished static HTML sites.

| Component | Technology | Role |
|-----------|-----------|------|
| Web framework | FastAPI + uvicorn | Async HTTP API and static file serving |
| Templating | Jinja2 | HTML page rendering from markdown content |
| Database | SQLite (async) | Project metadata and generation state |
| Build system | hatchling | Python package building |
| Package manager | uv | Dependency resolution and virtual environments |
| Code highlighting | highlight.js | Client-side syntax highlighting |
| Search | Client-side (lunr.js or similar) | In-browser full-text search |
| Markdown | Python markdown library | Markdown-to-HTML conversion |

---

## FastAPI

FastAPI serves as the core web framework, providing both the REST API for managing documentation generation and the static file serving layer for rendered documentation sites.

### Why FastAPI

FastAPI was chosen for its native async support, automatic OpenAPI documentation, and high performance via uvicorn. Since documentation generation involves long-running AI CLI subprocess calls, the async architecture allows the server to remain responsive while generation tasks execute in the background via `asyncio.to_thread()`.

### API Surface

docsfy exposes seven endpoints organized into three groups:

```
POST /api/generate              # Start doc generation for a repo URL
GET  /api/status                # List all projects and their generation status
GET  /api/projects/{name}       # Get project details (last generated, commit SHA, pages)
DELETE /api/projects/{name}     # Remove a project and its generated docs
GET  /api/projects/{name}/download  # Download site as .tar.gz archive
GET  /docs/{proje
